speaker: interviewer
text: You wish to tell us a story in Krakow that happened before you left for Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. First of all, when a Jew comes to a strange city, which he doesn't know anybody, he goes to synagogue. And I went to the synagogue, which I remember, very clearly, was in the first floor in [INAUDIBLE]. When I say a synagogue, it was a shtiebel, right? And they told me that the week before-- at that time, there was still lawlessness in Poland. You know, the people are being killed for no reason and so on-- and they took out 27 people out of the synagogue the week before, and they shot them-- the Polacks. Now, that's one story.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next story is my travels to Warsaw went as follows. I couldn't go from Krakow directly to Warsaw. I had to go to Katowice to catch the train to go to Warsaw, right, because that line was not open yet, from Krakow to Warsaw. And the trains were-- had no windows. They had no windows. And it was bitterly cold. And my mother was very clever because it was-- anti-Semitism was raging in there. And when they saw a Jew still alive, they couldn't believe it. They just could not believe that a Jew was still alive. We don't want you here. Hitler didn't kill you yet? Where did you escape, right? So she bought me a Polish coat from the army, an army coat. And she said, always have your-- your collar up. And she bought me such a cap, you know, a Polish military cap. And that's how I was traveling. And by the way, I was also inducted into the army. [LAUGHS] Right I-- you know, I didn't realize it, you know, only now when I look through the-- but I didn't go, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, what I remember about one trip was, I was sitting in a train. It was dark-- nighttime. And there was a young couple sitting opposite me. And somehow, Jews-- they smell one another, straight away. And straight away I saw them they're Jewish. They didn't know I was Jewish, but I saw them, that they are Jews. And I look at them. I said, I'm [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, where are you going? They said, we're going to Warsaw to fix up a few documents, because we're going to go to Melbourne. We're going to the Melbourne. They were newly married, and they were going to Melbourne. So I said, we're going to exchange you know, telephone-- you know, addresses. When we're going-- when I am in Sydney, we'll see each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the train used to stop very often on the way. The trip now by plane takes 50 minutes. By train, I think, it takes four hours-- you know, whole night-- you know, twelve hours or whatever. It was impossible. Anyway, the train stopped. It was pitch dark. And some hooligans came into the train. And they looked with torches. And they looked at these people. [SPEAKING POLISH], these are Jews. They took them out, right in front of me, on the embankment, they shot them dead. And this I'll never forget. A young couple survived the war, were going to Melbourne, and they got shot, you know, in Poland after the war. And I'm talking about '47. That's incredible. And it was a colossal anti-Semitism, colossal. Now I'm coming back to that Polack. And in one of those trips, in Warsaw, you know-- so I came in the morning. Used the trains to go at nighttime. I came in the morning to Warsaw. Straight away, I went to the various embassies where you had to go to get the papers, because I was fixing up for for my auntie, and for my two cousins, and my mother, and I. And I went straight there. And there were queues, and waiting, and money, and this, and the other one. And my mother used to give me a sandwich, you know, to eat on the way. You know, because there was no restaurants. There was nothing. And I finished the work once about 5, 6 o'clock in the evening. [? Nerves ?] were shot. And I had nothing else to do in Warsaw. You know, I finished what I wanted to achieve on that particular trip. And I go to the train, to get a train back to Katowice and back to Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was sitting in there, having a cup of tea. And comes a young man, beautifully dressed-- beautifully dressed. And he looks at me-- Joseph? I said, yes. [SPEAKING POLISH]! This Jew saved my life! I said, that's you? He said, that's me. He said, what are you doing in here? I said, I'm just taking the next train. I'm going to Krakow. Where is your mother? He loved my mother. My mother's in Krakow. He went and got himself a ticket, and we went back to Krakow together-- as he stood. I said, what happened to you? They took you off from that-- you know, from that-- you know, near [INAUDIBLE], you know? They took him to Moscow. They put him in jail in Moscow. And they checked out all the things which he told them-- which concentration camp he go, why he got out, and why he got in, and so on. And finally, they gave him a ticket to go back to Poland, right? Came back to Poland. He had no one. He found out his father was in the Polish army in London. And every ship which came with people repatriated, the father sent him money and he sent him, you know, goods. [LAUGHING] He sent him everything. So he became--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a rich man. And he came when I-- I'll never forget it. I opened up the door. And my mother was there, you know. And I came in. And mother-- who is this? And in disbelief, he lie down on the floor crying that we saved his life, right? So that's what I wanted to tell you about the Polish trip.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you eventually made the decision to come to Australia. You boarded the ship--

speaker: interviewee
text: But there was no other-- there was no decision. I mean, we couldn't make any decisions, because we couldn't go to Palestine. We wanted to go to Palestine, because Mendel-- my mother's brother, you know, my uncle-- was writing, you come to Haifa. There's a flat waiting for you. It's not mine, it's yours, and also Mina with the children. There's a flat waiting for you. But we couldn't get in. So we went to-- we came to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you find the culture here in Australia when you arrived, in comparison to what you experienced in Europe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Completely strange. It was-- I could not reconcile myself. Because when we went through the Panama Canal, all right-- so the Jewish people there were waiting for us. They gave us a big banquet. And they were crying that we are alive, and so on, you know. And we were saying, why are you crying? We should be laughing, you know? We were alive-- and such a warm atmosphere. We came in here. It was just the opposite. I mean, it's hard to speak about it. But you know, the Australian population-- to the Australian population, we were [? wogs. ?] They hated anybody who didn't speak English, or who looked a little bit different than they, or did thing-- or eat something different than they did. And they hated us. They hated us. But this was bearable. This, you could understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to tell us, you're going to take our jobs away. What are we going to do? We don't need you. This country's a happy country. We don't want you. Go back where you come from. That's how they-- and we used to tell them that when we start working, there'll be work for us. There'll be work for you. There'll be work for everybody. This was bearable. But the biggest anti-Semitism came from our Jewish people.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was the Anglo-Saxons. You know, the Great Synagogue had Anglo-Saxon people. And it's a very well documented fact. And one perhaps shouldn't speak about it, but if you ask me, I'm telling you, that there was a person called Saul Symonds. And he actually wrote a letter to the government asking them not to allow the Jewish people in. Only 25%-- say if a boat comes with 500 people, only 25% should be Jewish. It's documented, right. And only the next to the family-- you know, like father, mother, brother, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's a fellow called Fink who was in Melbourne. And he rang up to Arthur Calwell, asking him and begging him and crying that there's a ship coming with 500 people [INAUDIBLE], and they were going to send it back to Poland, to [INAUDIBLE], to allow these people to come to the country. So he allowed them. And there is a letter from Symonds, telling off Mr. Caldwell, why he allowed it without consulting him, right? So I could not understand these people here at all. I didn't get on with them very well at all. I just could not. And on top of it, you see, my uncle and his family had seats in the Great Synagogue. And I didn't want to go to synagogue. Because-- why I didn't want to go to synagogue is because all through the war, and after the war, I was blaming the [NON-ENGLISH], which his name was [? Halberstam, ?] that he told my grandfather not to leave Poland. And this was killing me, right? And from being very orthodox, I became the other way. And you know, then I started to question God and the whole thing. And on top of it, if there was a God in heaven, and my father was so pious, and he was so religious, and he observed all the 365 commandments, which was [INAUDIBLE], and my grandfather-- why did he get killed? Where was God? And then, you know, a Jewish person-- you know, the first thing a Hasid says is, [SPEAKING HEBREW]. You know, I believe in the coming of the Moshiac. And I couldn't believe that Moshiac was going to come. I just could not believe that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the young people again, my age group-- I had a very good experience. I lived in Coogee. And there was a fellow in there who came, a Russian Jew. He came. They had two daughters there. And they made me a party and they introduced me to all his friends. So I had a very good-- but the young people in here, my age group-- to me, they were completely empty handed-- empty headed. They had no-- they had no idea what was going on in the world. And if you told them something, which was going on, they were just not interested. They couldn't give a damn! They didn't give a damn what was going on in the world. All they were interested-- to have entertainment and to have fun. That's all they were interested in. I could not believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a very different way of life here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, it was completely [INAUDIBLE]. And on top of it, my uncle, you know which was-- came from Poland-- it was my uncle, my mother-- you know, he came from Poland as a young man in here. He became more Australian than the Australians. He became more [YIDDISH]. You know, that's a Yiddish saying. You know, he became more Pope than a Pope. And the first thing he told me was that I cannot live in Australia with a name like Weinreich. He was a wonderful man-- good hearted, wonderful man. He wanted the best for me. So I said, why can't I live like a Weinreich? Because nobody will be able to pronounce it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, what do you suggest, my dear uncle? What should I do? He said, your name is Weinreich. This is wine and reich is rich. So either we'll call you Mr. Rich, or we'll call you Mr. Wine. Now I said, look, I don't like wine, and I'm not rich. If I have got to change my name to Rich or to Weinrich, I'll take the next boat back to Poland. So you know what he told me? I'll never forget what he told me. He said, you will do very well in this country, you bastard, because you are stubborn. [LAUGHS] I got on very well with him later on. And he helped us a lot. So eventually you settled down here, you married, you found work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I settled down here. I met my wife. So my auntie, which was Australian, you know, once had a heart and heart conversation with me. She said, I want to introduce you to the finest families in Sydney. You want rich? Rich. You want poor? Poor. You want religious? Religious. I said, auntie, my wife I'll find myself. So what are you going to do? You don't like the girls in here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, no, I don't. I said, I'll go to Israel. I'll stay there for six, seven months, and I'll find myself a wife. I had friends in here. And they told me, my sister is coming from Israel for a visit, right? At the time we had a factory-- mattresses and furniture, you know, my uncle and I. Not this one, another uncle. So I went in there. And I measured it out, because they had a special nook, you know. And Saturday morning, I deliver the mattress. And I come down from there. And I see this girl coming up, this beautiful girl, coming up with the uncle and auntie, crying. And I came home. And my Auntie Mina lived opposite us, you know, in a [INAUDIBLE], you know? I was very close with Auntie Mina. I was more close with Auntie Mina than with my mother, because my mother was very possessive. You know, she wanted-- she lost her husband. She lost her son. She lost everybody, right? So she was very possessive. So I said, to auntie, auntie, I met my wife today. She said, you're crazy, meshuggah, you know, come and tell me the story. So anyway, she came next Saturday. And we started going out. There was no question about it. We're going to get married, you know. We-- I didn't even ask her to get married. And when were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 15th of November, '53.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have children as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: I've got the most glorious and the most beautiful children a family can have.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My eldest daughter is Lilian. She's an architect. And she's a wonderful painter. As you can see. There's lots of paintings in here, which Lilian has done-- very proud of her. And my second daughter is-- is a what's its name-- is a business lady. And my son is a lawyer, but he doesn't practice law. He's in business. Now, I just want to tell you quickly, because you're talking about marriage, I was working in Newtown. And I was going by tram. I was working in my uncle's factory, you know, to learn the trade. I was going by tram. On the right hand side was the University of Sydney. And I was always looking at the University of Sydney and saying, may God help me-- I didn't believe much in God, but anyway. But I said, may God help me one day, when I get married, that my children should go into this university, and finish this university. And I must tell you, all three of them went to that university. And they all finished with honors. And this was the happiest moment in my life was-- I had a few happy moments, of course-- when I got married, when my son was bar mitzvah. And all three children, when I saw them walking around-- they were getting their degrees, with their hats-- of course, this is completely strange to us, you know, hats with [NON-ENGLISH], you know. And they were tipping their hats to the professor there. You know, they-- that was the most happiest moment of my life. That was a realization of a dream that is incredible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Weinreich, I want to thank you for the time, for the interview. In closing, is there anything that you would like to add, or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, just a very short one, that I am a very lucky man, that I went through a war, and I met such a wonderful person as Aneta. She is the most glorious wife and the most glorious person that you can come across-- full of wisdom, full of strength, full of vitality. We're so well-matched together that it's just unbelievable. You know, I was right when I said I'm going to marry her. She didn't even know that I was going to marry her. [LAUGHS] But anyway, and I have got the most glorious, and the most beautiful, and most lovable children that a man can ever, ever imagine. And for this I am grateful. I don't know to who I should be grateful. But I am grateful that out of the ashes, right, I have risen like a falcon. And nothing, nothing-- you know, we have got tremendous strength of character. We are-- nothing can stress me. Nothing can throw me. I can handle any situation, because I've got always this behind in my mind-- what my wife went through and what I went through. You know, and we didn't need any psychiatrists, or any psychologists, or anybody like this. Ourselves-- we, ourselves-- we helped one another. You know, and we became completely normal people and putting all this behind us. And the children-- they never felt it. They never felt it. They were brought up in an atmosphere of love, and beauty, and elegance.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife has got tremendous taste. And she is an artist, in a way, right? And to come to such a point is-- I don't want to use the same sentence that my wife used, because I heard her saying it, but I also have got to say, [SPEAKING HEBREW], that we have lived to this moment. Thank you very much. We'll take a break.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Weinreich, would you like to introduce the lovely lady sitting next to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The champion. [LAUGHS] This is my wife, Aneta, the most beloved lady in town, OK? In the world, I should say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the two of you have brought up three lovely children into Australia. After all that you've both been through-- because I understand that you're also a survivor-- how have you-- how do you think that the experiences of what you've been through affected your lives in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we're very-- first of all, when I arrived in Australia, it will be-- I arrived on the 27th of March to Australia. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: In '53. And the next day, I met my husband. So that was one of my very happy moments in life. And then we got married. And we have a very, very happy married life. And we are married for 42 years. We never quarrel. We understand life. And we know what to expect from life. The experiences, what we went through, does help us a lot. Because it strengthened us. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK. It's OK. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we are strong people, but yet very soft. [CRYING] That's how we are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two sides to the story-- we're strong on one side, but my wife is very emotional, you know? When it comes to things, still she becomes very emotional. And I think that made us very, very strong. And we can take any stress in our way, which is given in our way, and we overcome it. We overcome it.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is not a thing that we will not overcome.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't have it-- we didn't have it easy in Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mind you, we worked very hard for every cent we've got. We worked very hard, worked day and night, in bringing up three children, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And especially when we see people, you know, low down, we always stretch our hand. Because we know what it means-- you know, hardship, or you know-- so we try to help other people, if possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have people in Australia approached you, interested to know about what you've been through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Not even the family which survived, and which were not during the war in Poland, and which were here in Australia, or were in India, or somewhere else-- they never asked us how we survived or how the family died.

speaker: interviewee
text: It is a great difference between the survivor and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Even in the same family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the same family, and between people in same family that didn't survive the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: That survived the war, but they were not in Poland during the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, exactly. So it is a-- I feel it is a vast difference and completely different wavelengths, up to that point that we sometimes don't understand each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your children have learned from your experience?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, no question about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They have got a full understanding, absolutely. They understand what we went through. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: They're marvelous people, marvelous human beings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and the way we are, they understand us more than we understand ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, the not only love we get from them, but the respect they give us-- it's just indescribable. They keep a tab on us. You know, the things have reversed. Instead of looking after the children, the children look after us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now when my wife says-- I just want to quickly, if we've got-- how much more have we got? One minute, two minutes? When we came to Australia, it was a normal thing to help one another. You know, people came from overseas that couldn't speak English, didn't have a job. It was a normal thing. I just want to tell one story and that's it. Her mother has got Alzheimer's. And we believe in going to the top people. So I found out who is the top man in Alzheimer diseases. So they tell me there's a Professor Henry Brodaty. Now I said, this rings a bell. Just let me think who he is. And I remembered. Because I-- when I came to Australia after one year, people used to come more often, you know-- after I was here a year, I was already learned English. And people after me came in droves later on. And I was-- I was known as the big [NON-ENGLISH], you know? I was known as the big [NON-ENGLISH], you know, because I spoke English, and they couldn't speak English. Right, so I was looking for jobs for them, and so forth, and houses to live in, and flats to live in, and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my wife, this rings a bell. Let me think. And I remembered. On the second-- why I remembered was a friend of mine rang me up on the second day of Rosh Hashanah. And although I'm-- you know, the God story is not so kosher, but I don't travel on Rosh Hashanah or Yom Kippur. And he said, there's a family in Cremorne, which if we don't give them a hand, they'll die. It's a husband and a wife and a small child. And they'll die. And I said, OK. Rosh Hashanah will be over. I've got a truck. We'll go and see them. No, no, no. He said, you know, even a doctor is obliged to see after people, even in Shabbat, to break the Shabbat, he meant. We're going now. And I'm more religious than you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in there. And I looked. And these people lived really in a [INAUDIBLE]. They lived in a [INAUDIBLE]. You could see people walking in there. The husband was sick, and the mother was sick, and the little boy of three was sick, and he was snorting. Sit down, let me see what we can do. First of all, I went to the corner shop, and I bought food-- right, bread, and butter, and cheese, and everything else, and put it on the table-- eat. The boy needs a wash. I went upstairs, and I hear a child crying, and I knocked on the door, and I say, listen, give me something to wash the boy. So they gave me-- Australian people, wonderful people-- gave me a-- and I washed the boy. I said, now what do we need? I need a job and I need some place to live. OK. So I said, job? No problem. You can have 10 jobs tomorrow morning. Because there was a shortage of people. I've got a cousin that was a [INAUDIBLE] who has got a factory of knitting. I'll get you a job. And what about-- so the wife says she also needs a job, but she needs to put the boy in the kindergarten. So I looked up in telephone book, and I got him into the kindergarten. I carried him to the kindergarten. And he was knocking me. And he was pushing me, you know, and I got him to kindergarten. And then I said to my wife, you know what? That name rings a bell. And I ring up to Mr. Brodaty-- to Professor Brodaty in hospital. And I said, I would like to speak to him. And two days later, they-- you know, he's busy, you know. Two days later, he rings me up. What can I do for you? I said, my mother-in-law, I think-- I'm not a doctor. I think she's got Alzheimer's. And you're the specialist in Alzheimer. Help me. He said, I am not looking after private people. I am looking after-- you know, he's got the whole Alzheimer's disease, you know, in Australia-- he's got it in his hands, right? I'll give you a good colleague, who will look after her. I said, no, no, no. His father died from Alzheimer's. And I said, you ring up to your mother now. I said, and you say, Weinreich is calling. He needs help. And I said to my wife, 10 minutes later, if he catch his mother, he'll ring me back. He rings me back and he said, what can I do for you? That is the Professor Brodaty, which I carried to the kindergarten.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he did help us quite a lot with my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was marvelous to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a very nice story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a cent did I have to pay him. And I wouldn't pay him anyway, if he asked me for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He to our home, because my mother wouldn't go to hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: He came here.

speaker: interviewee
text: She would not go to any-- she would not [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: He is the biggest man in his field.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we-- as my wife said, we're strong. We're tough. But in the same time, when it comes to [NON-ENGLISH], there is no such a thing as not to help--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: --other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We also like to thank Steven Spielberg that prompted us to tell our story, you know, holocaust story, to perpetuate it. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: So what are you crying now for? Love. Smart.

speaker: interviewee
text: So our children will be able to refer to the story and know exactly what we went through. And I have got a separate tape. And they will--

speaker: interviewee
text: They will look at it with a great lot of fondness, and happiness, and you know-- and gratefulness. And everything is going to be OK, not to worry.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
